section.title: Elijah Taken Up
section.sectionRationale: This chapter closes Elijah's earthly ministry and opens Elisha's. A final journey with three farewells, a parted Jordan, a whirlwind ascension, and a fallen cloak transfer the prophetic office. Elisha's first acts, healing water and cursing mockers, immediately display the same LORD at work through a new servant.
section.thesis: The LORD who took Elijah up in fire and wind does not leave Israel without a prophet, but doubles His Spirit on Elisha, whose first miracles prove that the God of Elijah is still present and active in Israel.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the final journey and threefold farewell, verses 1 to 8; the request for a double portion and the ascension, verses 9 to 14; the sons of the prophets confirm Elisha, verses 15 to 18; and Elisha's first two signs, healed water and cursed mockers, verses 19 to 25.

units.label: The final journey
units.note: as surely as the LORD lives, I will not leave you

verses.text: Shortly before the LORD took Elijah up to heaven in a whirlwind, Elijah and Elisha were on their way from Gilgal,

and Elijah said to Elisha, "Please stay here, for the LORD has sent me on to Bethel." But Elisha replied, "As surely as the LORD lives and as you yourself live, I will not leave you." So they went down to Bethel.
Then the {{nabi|sons of the prophets}} at Bethel came out to Elisha and said, "Do you know that the LORD will take your master away from you today?" "Yes, I know," he replied. "Do not speak of it."

And Elijah said to Elisha, "Please stay here, for the LORD has sent me on to Jericho." But Elisha replied, "As surely as the LORD lives and as you yourself live, I will not leave you." So they went to Jericho.
Then the sons of the prophets at Jericho came up to Elisha and said, "Do you know that the LORD will take your master away from you today?" "Yes, I know," he replied. "Do not speak of it."

And Elijah said to Elisha, "Please stay here, for the LORD has sent me on to the Jordan." But Elisha replied, "As surely as the LORD lives and as you yourself live, I will not leave you." So the two of them went on.
Then a company of fifty of the sons of the prophets went and stood at a distance, facing Elijah and Elisha as the two of them stood by the Jordan.
And Elijah took his {{addereth|cloak}}, rolled it up, and struck the waters, which parted to the right and to the left, so that the two of them crossed over on dry ground.

units.label: A double portion and the whirlwind

verses.text: After they had crossed over, Elijah said to Elisha, "Tell me, what can I do for you before I am taken away from you?" "Please, let me inherit a double portion of your {{ruach|spirit}}," Elisha replied.
"You have requested a difficult thing," said Elijah. "Nevertheless, if you see me as I am taken from you, it will be yours. But if not, then it will not be so."
As they were walking along and talking together, suddenly a chariot of fire with horses of fire appeared and separated the two of them, and Elijah went up into heaven in a whirlwind.
As Elisha watched, he cried out, "My father, my father, the chariots and horsemen of Israel!" And he saw Elijah no more. So taking hold of his own clothes, he tore them in two.
Elisha also picked up the cloak that had fallen from Elijah, and he went back and stood on the bank of the Jordan.

Then he took the cloak of Elijah that had fallen from him and struck the waters. "Where now is the LORD, the God of Elijah?" he asked. And when he had struck the waters, they parted to the right and to the left, and Elisha crossed over.

units.label: The sons of the prophets confirm Elisha
units.note: the spirit of Elijah rests on Elisha

verses.text: When the sons of the prophets who were watching him from Jericho saw what had happened, they said, "The spirit of Elijah rests on Elisha." And they went to meet him and bowed down to the ground before him.

"Look now," they said to Elisha, "we your servants have fifty valiant men. Please let them go and search for your master. Perhaps the Spirit of the LORD has taken him up and put him on one of the mountains or in one of the valleys." "Do not send them," Elisha replied.
But when they pressed him to the point of embarrassment, he said, "Send them." And they sent fifty men, who searched for three days but did not find Elijah.
When they returned to Elisha, who was staying in Jericho, he said to them, "Didn't I tell you not to go?"
Then the men of the city said to Elisha, "Please note, our lord, that the city's location is good, as you can see. But the water is bad and the land is unfruitful."
"Bring me a new bowl," he replied, "and put some {{melach|salt}} in it." So they brought it to him,
and Elisha went out to the spring, cast the salt into it, and said, "This is what the LORD says: 'I have healed this water. No longer will it cause death or unfruitfulness.'"
And the waters there have been healthy to this day, according to the word spoken by Elisha.
From there, Elisha went up to Bethel, and as he was walking up the road, a group of boys came out of the city and jeered at him, chanting, "Go up, you baldhead! Go up, you baldhead!"
Then he turned around, looked at them, and called down a curse on them in the name of the LORD. Suddenly two female bears came out of the woods and mauled forty-two of the boys.
And Elisha went on to Mount Carmel, and from there he returned to Samaria.

nabi.gloss: Sons of the prophets, guild communities of prophets living at Bethel, Jericho, and elsewhere under a recognized master like Elijah or Elisha. Their repeated question to Elisha shows the coming transition was already known and watched throughout Israel's prophetic circles.

addereth.gloss: Cloak or mantle, the same garment Elijah wrapped Elisha in in 1 Kings 19:19 to call him. Here it parts the Jordan in Elijah's hand and again in Elisha's, visibly transferring the prophetic office from master to successor.

ruach.gloss: Spirit. Elisha's bold request for a double portion of Elijah's ruach echoes the double share of inheritance given a firstborn son, Deuteronomy 21:17, asking not for more power than Elijah but to be marked as his true successor and heir in ministry.

melach.gloss: Salt. Elisha casts salt, ordinarily a preservative associated with covenant faithfulness, Leviticus 2:13, into a poisoned spring, and the LORD turns death into life, a small but vivid picture of restoration through His prophet.

basic.context: Elijah's ministry ends as it began, in dramatic encounter with God. On his last journey he tells Elisha three times to stay behind, and three times Elisha refuses to leave him. At the Jordan, Elijah parts the water with his cloak, and after Elisha asks for a double portion of his spirit, a chariot and horses of fire sweep Elijah up in a whirlwind. Elisha, left with the fallen cloak, parts the same river and is recognized by the prophetic community as Elijah's true successor. He immediately heals poisoned water at Jericho and, when a group of youths mock him and, by extension, the LORD's authority in him, they suffer a fierce judgment.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a unique, unrepeated transfer of prophetic office; Elisha's story is not a pattern for every believer to expect a visible ascension or an army of bears. What endures is God's faithfulness to provide for His people even when a beloved leader is taken, and His concern to vindicate His servant's authority before a watching nation.
basic.originalLanguages: The nevi'im, the prophetic communities at Bethel and Jericho, already sense the coming change and press Elisha with it. Elisha requests a double portion of Elijah's ruach, spirit, language recalling a firstborn's inheritance, not asking for more power than Elijah but to be received as his true heir. The transferred addereth, cloak, becomes the visible sign that his request is granted.
basic.typology: Elijah's ascension in a whirlwind, without seeing death, together with the passing of his spirit to a successor who continues and even expands his ministry, anticipates the pattern of Christ's own ascension and the sending of the Spirit to His disciples so that His works continue and increase after He is taken up.
basic.christ: Just as Elijah ascended and left his spirit doubled on Elisha so that his ministry continued through another, Christ ascended to the Father and sent His Spirit so that His disciples would do even greater works, John 14:12, Acts 1:8 to 9. Elisha crossing the parted Jordan and being welcomed as Elijah's heir foreshadows how believers, indwelt by the Spirit of the ascended Christ, carry forward His mission on earth.
basic.god: God provides continuity for His people; no leader, however great, is indispensable to His purposes, because He raises up others empowered by His own Spirit. He also publicly vindicates the honor due His prophet and, through him, His own name, showing that mocking God's appointed messenger is no small thing.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 19:19-21 records Elijah first casting his cloak over Elisha, the beginning of the relationship completed here. 2 Kings 13:14 has Jehoash later use the very words Elisha cries here, my father, my father, the chariots and horsemen of Israel, of Elisha himself. Acts 1:9-11 parallels Christ's own ascension before witnesses.
basic.oneStory: As Elijah's era closes in fire and wind, God does not abandon His people but doubles His Spirit on a successor whose ministry immediately proves the same LORD is at work, a pattern completed when the ascended Christ pours out His Spirit on His church so that His mission continues until He returns.

context.literary: The chapter is carefully structured around threes, three towns, three refusals to leave, before the climactic ascension, then mirrors the crossing of the Jordan on both sides of the transition, and closes with two brief but pointed episodes that establish Elisha's authority before the wider narrative of his ministry unfolds.
context.historical: Bethel, Jericho, and the Jordan were each significant sites in Israel's history, from the patriarchs to the conquest under Joshua; retracing this route as his final journey ties Elijah's departure to the whole sweep of Israel's story. Guilds of prophets, bene ha-nevi'im, are attested at several of these towns, suggesting organized prophetic communities under recognized leadership.
context.canonical: This chapter closes the Elijah cycle begun in 1 Kings 17 and opens the Elisha cycle that dominates 2 Kings 2 through 13. Elijah's fiery ascension becomes proverbial, later invoked at Malachi 4:5 to 6 and fulfilled typologically in John the Baptist, Luke 1:17, and Elijah himself reappears at the Transfiguration, Matthew 17:3.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the transfer of prophetic authority from Elijah to Elisha as a work of God rather than human succession, and to show through Elisha's first miracles that the LORD who worked through Elijah is equally present and active through his successor.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a unique, unrepeated historical event, Elijah's bodily ascension and Elisha's confirmed succession; it is not a template for every ministry transition, nor does the judgment on the mockers license believers to curse those who insult them. What endures is God's provision of continued leadership for His people and the seriousness of despising His appointed messengers.

debates.question: Was the mauling of the youths in verses 23 to 24 a proportionate response to childish mockery?

views.label: A judgment on a wider act of contempt for the LORD's prophet and covenant order
views.case: The BSB footnote allows the group to be older youths or young men rather than small children, and their taunt, go up, mockingly dares Elisha to ascend as Elijah had, treating the ascension and the prophetic office itself with public contempt in a town, Bethel, already notorious for idolatry; the judgment answers a serious covenantal offense, not innocent teasing.

views.label: A troubling text that displays God's holiness rather than commending Elisha's temperament
views.case: Even granting the youths were not small children, the severity of the punishment, forty two mauled, is startling to modern readers, and some read it as showing the LORD's own zeal for His prophet's honor at the very start of Elisha's ministry, a severity mercy later moderates as Elisha's story unfolds with far more healing than judgment.

debates.bearing: Both views take the text seriously as historical judgment rather than an ordinary lesson in etiquette, and both note the emphasis on establishing Elisha's God given authority at the outset of his ministry.

study.originalLanguages: The bene ha-nevi'im, the prophetic communities, already know what is coming and repeatedly test Elisha with it, while Elisha's own request, a double portion of Elijah's ruach, spirit, echoes the language of a firstborn's inheritance rather than a bid for greater power. The addereth, the cloak, becomes the visible token of that inheritance, first parting the Jordan for Elijah, then for Elisha, silently confirming to the watching prophets that the spirit of Elijah now rests on Elisha.

typology.type: The prophet taken up in fire and wind without dying, his spirit passed to a successor
typology.fulfillment: Christ's ascension to the Father and His sending of the Holy Spirit so His disciples continue and extend His ministry, John 16:7, Acts 1:8-11.

typology.type: The Jordan parted twice, marking entry into a new phase of ministry
typology.fulfillment: Baptism, in which believers pass through death to new life and are commissioned to carry on Christ's mission, Romans 6:4.

study.christ: Elijah is taken up bodily while his spirit rests doubled on Elisha so that the same works, and more, continue in Israel; so the ascended Christ sent His Spirit upon His disciples, promising they would do even greater works because He goes to the Father, John 14:12. Elisha standing at the Jordan asking, where now is the LORD, the God of Elijah, and finding the waters part just as before, pictures every believer's confidence that the same God who worked through Christ still works through His Spirit filled people today.
study.god: God provides for the continuation of His purposes beyond any single servant, however great, by pouring out His Spirit on a successor. He is also jealous for the honor of His appointed prophet, publicly vindicating Elisha's authority both through the healed spring, a sign of blessing, and the judgment on the mockers, a sign that contempt for His messenger is contempt for Him.

crossReferences.note: Elijah first casts his cloak over Elisha, calling him to follow and serve, the beginning of the relationship completed at the Jordan here.
King Jehoash later cries the same words over the dying Elisha, showing Elisha fully inherited Elijah's honored title and role.
Elijah becomes the pattern for a promised forerunner, fulfilled typologically in John the Baptist, Luke 1:17.
Christ's own ascension before witnesses, followed by the sending of the Spirit, parallels the pattern of departure and empowered succession here.

study.oneStory: God does not leave His people without a witness; as Elijah is carried up in fire, his spirit rests doubled on Elisha, whose first acts prove the same LORD is present and active in Israel. This pattern, a departing servant and a Spirit-filled successor who carries the mission forward, anticipates the ascension of Christ Himself, who sends His Spirit so that His church continues His work until He comes again.

quiz.q: How did Elisha respond each time Elijah told him to stay behind?

options.text: He refused, insisting three times, as surely as the LORD lives and as you yourself live, I will not leave you.
options.note: Verses 2, 4, and 6 record this identical refusal at Bethel, Jericho, and the Jordan.

options.text: He obeyed and waited behind at each town.
options.note: The text records the opposite; Elisha followed Elijah at every stage.

options.text: He argued with Elijah about where to go next.
options.note: Elisha did not argue over the destination, only insisted on staying with Elijah.

options.text: He left Elijah at Bethel to visit the sons of the prophets.
options.note: Elisha stayed with Elijah the entire journey, contrary to this option.

quiz.q: What did Elisha ask for before Elijah was taken from him, and how does that request compare to Elijah's own portion?

options.text: A double portion of Elijah's spirit, language echoing a firstborn son's inheritance rather than a claim to greater power than Elijah himself.
options.note: Verse 9 records the request, and the double portion reflects the inheritance right of a firstborn heir, Deuteronomy 21:17.

options.text: To be taken up to heaven in the same way as Elijah.
options.note: Elisha asks for a double portion of spirit, not for his own bodily ascension.

options.text: Silver and gold to establish his own household.
options.note: The request concerns spiritual inheritance, not material wealth.

options.text: Permission to return home and abandon the prophetic office.
options.note: Elisha's request shows deep commitment to continuing the prophetic ministry, not abandoning it.

quiz.q: How was Elijah taken from Elisha, and what confirmed that his request was granted?

options.text: A chariot and horses of fire appeared and Elijah went up in a whirlwind; Elisha then parted the Jordan with Elijah's fallen cloak just as Elijah had.
options.note: Verses 11 and 14 record the ascension and the confirming sign at the Jordan.

options.text: Elijah simply walked into the wilderness and disappeared from view.
options.note: The text specifically describes a fiery chariot and a whirlwind, a far more dramatic departure.

options.text: Elijah died of natural causes and was buried by Elisha.
options.note: Elijah did not die; he was taken up alive in the whirlwind.

options.text: Nothing confirmed that Elisha's request had been granted.
options.note: The parted Jordan and the prophets' own recognition, verse 15, confirmed it clearly.

quiz.q: What did the sons of the prophets at Jericho conclude when they saw Elisha part the Jordan?

options.text: They said, the spirit of Elijah rests on Elisha, and bowed down before him.
options.note: Verse 15 records their explicit recognition and act of homage.

options.text: They doubted Elisha and refused to acknowledge him.
options.note: The text shows immediate recognition and honor, not doubt.

options.text: They demanded Elisha prove himself further before accepting him.
options.note: Their initial search for Elijah in verses 16 to 18 was not a test of Elisha but a lingering hope for their former master.

options.text: They ignored the event entirely.
options.note: They responded immediately with recognition and bowed before Elisha.

quiz.q: What were Elisha's first two recorded acts of ministry after Elijah's departure?

options.text: He healed the poisoned water at Jericho by casting in salt, then pronounced a curse on youths who mocked him at Bethel, resulting in their mauling by bears.
options.note: Verses 19 to 24 record both signs in sequence, one of blessing and one of judgment.

options.text: He rebuilt the altar at Bethel and offered sacrifices.
options.note: No altar building is recorded here; his acts were healing water and pronouncing judgment.

options.text: He raised a dead child to life and multiplied oil.
options.note: Those miracles occur later, in chapter 4, not in this chapter.

options.text: He performed no miracles until much later in his ministry.
options.note: The text records these two miracles immediately following Elijah's ascension.

questions: Elisha refused to leave Elijah's side no matter how many times he was told to stay behind. What costly persistence is God calling you to show in following Him or a mentor He has placed in your life?

Elisha asked for a double portion of Elijah's spirit so he could carry on the work, not to make a name for himself. Where is God inviting you to ask boldly for what you need to serve faithfully rather than settling for less?

When Elijah was taken, Elisha picked up the fallen cloak and stepped forward into ministry rather than despairing. What has God left in your hands to pick up and carry forward after a loss or a transition?
The prophets recognized God's hand on Elisha because of what they saw Him do, not merely what Elisha claimed. How is your life offering visible evidence of God's presence to those watching you?
This chapter shows both remarkable blessing, healed water, and severe judgment, mocked authority. How do you hold together God's kindness and His holiness in your own understanding of who He is?

translationNotes.note: A double portion recalls the inheritance law of Deuteronomy 21:17, where the firstborn receives twice the share of other heirs; Elisha is asking to be received as Elijah's true prophetic heir, not to exceed him in raw power.
The BSB footnote notes the group rendered 'boys' may equally mean small youths or insignificant young men, a detail bearing on how the severity of the judgment in verse 24 is understood.

Bethel was already associated with idolatrous worship centers, 1 Kings 12:28-33, making mockery of the LORD's prophet there a pointed act of contempt for God's authority in a place already given to false worship.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 2:1-25. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
